past_medical_history: Relapsing-remitting multiple sclerosis diagnosed 12 years ago
Prior optic neuritis right eye 10 years ago (residual mild right optic disc pallor, recovered vision)
Prior transverse myelitis at T6 level 8 years ago (residual brisk lower extremity reflexes, mild vibratory sense reduction in feet)
Last MS relapse 4 years ago (left arm numbness, treated with IV methylprednisolone, full recovery)
Stable on natalizumab for 6 years, no new MRI activity in 3 years
Depression diagnosed 5 years ago, partially treated

medications.frequency: As needed, up to twice daily

family_history: Father with coronary artery disease, deceased at 72
clinical_history.social_history: Occupational therapist, currently on disability leave for 6 months. Married 18 years, significant marital conflict over past 6 months related to disability insurance application and financial strain. Two children (ages 14 and 11). Former smoker (quit 8 years ago). No alcohol or drug use. Active in MS support group.

presenting_complaint.chief_complaint: New weakness in both legs -- I think I'm having another MS relapse
presenting_complaint.hpi: 45-year-old right-handed Caucasian female with a 12-year history of relapsing-remitting MS (on natalizumab, stable for 3 years on MRI) presenting with 2-week progressive bilateral leg weakness and numbness. The patient is convinced this represents a new MS relapse. However, the symptom pattern differs significantly from her prior documented relapses: (1) symptoms fluctuate dramatically throughout the day, with periods of normal ambulation alternating with inability to walk, (2) weakness is bilateral and symmetric (prior relapses were always unilateral), (3) symptoms worsen with emotional stress (notably after an argument with her husband about the disability insurance application) and improve with distraction, (4) she has preserved awareness throughout episodes. She recently learned from her neurologist that her MS may eventually transition to secondary progressive disease, causing significant anxiety about her future functional capacity. She has been on disability leave for 6 months and is currently applying for permanent disability benefits.
presenting_complaint.severity: Variable: walks normally at times, uses wheelchair at others
associated_symptoms: Bilateral leg numbness with sharp demarcation at the trunk
Bilateral leg heaviness that fluctuates dramatically

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert and oriented x4. GCS 15. Anxious on interview. Frequently mentions concern about MS progression. MMSE 30/30.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: CN II: Mild right optic disc pallor (OLD finding from prior optic neuritis 10 years ago, UNCHANGED). Visual acuity 20/25 right, 20/20 left (STABLE). Tubular visual fields on confrontation testing bilaterally (does not expand with increased testing distance — inconsistent with organic field loss). RAPD trace right (OLD, from prior optic neuritis). CN III-XII otherwise intact.
neurological_exam.motor: Upper extremities: 5/5 bilaterally, normal tone. Lower extremities: BILATERAL give-way weakness — initial contraction 4/5 that collapses abruptly to 0/5 on formal testing. Pattern is collapsing, not pyramidal. When asked to hurry to the bathroom, walks heel-to-toe normally without difficulty. Positive Hoover sign bilaterally. Tone normal in lower extremities (no spasticity despite old UMN lesion).
neurological_exam.sensory: Non-dermatomal sensory loss bilateral lower extremities with midline splitting at the trunk (sharp demarcation at umbilicus that splits vibration on the sternum). OLD findings STABLE: mild vibratory sense reduction at bilateral great toes (from prior T6 myelitis, documented as stable on prior exam 6 months ago).
neurological_exam.reflexes: Bilateral lower extremity reflexes 3+ (OLD finding from prior thoracic myelitis, STABLE from last examination 6 months ago). Bilateral upper extremity reflexes 2+. Plantar responses equivocal bilaterally (previously documented as equivocal).
neurological_exam.coordination: Normal finger-to-nose bilaterally. Heel-to-shin: when tested formally, appears clumsy bilaterally. When walking quickly, leg coordination is normal.
neurological_exam.gait: Dragging bilateral gait with inconsistent foot clearance in the exam room. When asked to walk quickly to catch an elevator, gait normalizes completely with full heel-toe pattern and appropriate arm swing. Positive chair test (rises from chair without difficulty when distracted).
neurological_exam.special_signs: Positive Hoover sign bilaterally. Give-way (collapsing) weakness pattern. Tubular visual fields. Midline splitting of vibration. Gait inconsistency with distraction. NOTE: OLD MS findings (right optic pallor, brisk LE reflexes, mild vibration reduction in toes) are STABLE and UNCHANGED from last documented exam 6 months ago.

findings.location: Periventricular white matter bilaterally, juxtacortical left frontal and right parietal, corpus callosum (Dawson fingers pattern)
findings.size: Multiple lesions, largest 12 mm in periventricular region

signal_characteristics.T1: Some lesions hypointense (T1 black holes), consistent with chronic axonal loss
signal_characteristics.DWI: No restricted diffusion in any lesion
signal_characteristics.post_gadolinium: NO enhancing lesions. No new or active inflammation.

additional_observations: Comparison with MRI from 6 months ago: NO new lesions identified
NO enhancing lesions on post-gadolinium imaging — no evidence of active MS inflammation
No cord compression or canal stenosis
Brain volume appears stable (no significant atrophy progression)
mri.impression: Stable MS disease burden on brain and spine MRI. Multiple known periventricular, juxtacortical, and corpus callosum lesions without new or enhancing lesions. Stable T6 cord lesion without enhancement. No evidence of active inflammatory disease. No new MRI activity to suggest MS relapse.
recommended_actions: Clinical correlation — MRI does not support a new MS relapse
Consider non-inflammatory etiology for current symptoms

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: 10 Hz alpha rhythm, symmetric, well-organized, reactive

eeg.impression: Normal EEG. No epileptiform activity. No focal slowing.

Inflammatory_Markers.clinical_significance: Normal; argues against active systemic inflammation

Other.clinical_significance: Positive JCV antibody with elevated index relevant for PML risk stratification on natalizumab. Patient has been on natalizumab >2 years with positive JCV; PML risk requires ongoing monitoring.
Low-normal vitamin D; supplementation recommended for MS patients

labs.interpretation: Normal CBC, BMP, CRP, and TSH. Anti-JCV antibody positive (relevant for natalizumab PML risk monitoring but does not explain current symptoms). Vitamin D borderline low. No laboratory evidence of active systemic inflammation or metabolic derangement to explain current symptoms.
abnormal_values_summary: Anti-JCV antibody positive (index 2.8) — PML risk monitoring on natalizumab

Visual_Evoked_Potentials.clinical_significance: Prolonged P100 latency in right eye — OLD finding from prior optic neuritis 10 years ago. STABLE compared to prior VEP from 2 years ago (126 ms).
Normal left eye P100 latency, unchanged from prior study
Significant interocular asymmetry consistent with prior right optic nerve demyelination — OLD finding, STABLE

output.interpretation: Visual evoked potentials demonstrate prolonged right eye P100 latency (128 ms), an OLD finding from prior optic neuritis 10 years ago. This is stable compared to the prior VEP study from 2 years ago. Left eye VEP is normal. No NEW changes. These findings do not indicate new MS activity.
abnormal_values_summary: Right eye P100 latency 128 ms (prolonged — OLD finding from prior optic neuritis, STABLE)

Somatosensory_Evoked_Potentials.clinical_significance: Mildly prolonged central conduction time — OLD finding consistent with prior thoracic myelitis at T6. STABLE compared to prior SSEP from 3 years ago.
Cortical response PRESENT — intact sensory pathway to cortex

output.interpretation: SSEPs from bilateral tibial nerves demonstrate mildly prolonged central conduction times bilaterally (P37 latencies 45.8 and 46.2 ms), which are OLD findings consistent with the known T6 thoracic cord lesion from prior myelitis. These are STABLE compared to prior SSEP study from 3 years ago. Critically, cortical responses are PRESENT bilaterally with adequate amplitude, indicating that the sensory pathways from the lower extremities to the cortex are intact despite the old demyelinating lesion. No NEW abnormalities. The presence of cortical responses argues against a new structural lesion in the sensory pathway as the cause of the patient's current sensory complaints.
abnormal_values_summary: Bilateral tibial P37 latencies mildly prolonged (OLD findings from prior T6 myelitis, STABLE)
Cortical responses PRESENT bilaterally — intact sensory pathway

findings.type: Side-by-side MRI comparison: current vs 6 months ago

signal_characteristics.lesion_count: Brain: 14 lesions identified, unchanged from prior. Spine: 1 lesion at T6, unchanged.
signal_characteristics.new_lesions: NONE. Zero new lesions on T2/FLAIR.
signal_characteristics.enhancing_lesions: NONE. Zero enhancing lesions on post-gadolinium imaging.
signal_characteristics.lesion_volume: Stable total lesion volume (no change within measurement error)
signal_characteristics.cord_lesion: T6 lesion unchanged in size, signal, and morphology

volumetrics.total_brain_lesion_volume: 4.2 mL (stable from 4.1 mL, within measurement variability)
volumetrics.brain_volume_change: No significant change in brain parenchymal fraction

additional_observations: Formal side-by-side comparison with images from 6 months ago confirms absolute stability
No change in lesion count, size, or enhancement pattern
No evidence of NEDA-3 failure (no new relapses, no new MRI activity, no disability progression attributable to MS)
output.impression: Formal comparison MRI confirms completely stable MS disease burden. Zero new or enhancing lesions on brain or spine imaging. No evidence of active MS inflammation. The current clinical symptoms cannot be attributed to new MS inflammatory activity based on MRI.
recommended_actions: Clinical symptoms are not explained by new MS activity on MRI
Consider alternative etiology for current presentation
Continue natalizumab and scheduled MRI monitoring

Psychiatric_Assessment.clinical_significance: Moderate depression with prominent fatigue, sleep disturbance, and anhedonia
Moderate anxiety with significant health anxiety and catastrophizing
Significant health anxiety specifically related to MS progression fears

Psychiatric_Assessment.value: Marital conflict (husband frustrated with disability claim), financial strain, role loss (cannot work as OT), fear of MS progression to SPMS, fear of becoming wheelchair-dependent
Psychiatric_Assessment.clinical_significance: Multiple concurrent psychosocial stressors temporally correlating with symptom onset

Psychiatric_Assessment.value: Patient firmly attributes all new symptoms to MS relapse. Expresses frustration that MRI is 'not showing the damage.' Reports feeling that clinicians are 'not believing' her. Resistant to psychological explanations.
Psychiatric_Assessment.clinical_significance: Strong biomedical illness attribution that may pose a barrier to accepting FND diagnosis

output.interpretation: Psychiatric evaluation reveals moderate depression (PHQ-9 = 16) and moderate anxiety (GAD-7 = 14) with prominent health anxiety related to MS progression. The patient's recent learning about potential secondary progressive MS transition has caused significant catastrophizing and fear. Multiple concurrent psychosocial stressors (marital conflict, financial strain, disability, role loss) temporally correlate with the onset of the new neurological symptoms. These psychological factors are both precipitants and perpetuating factors for functional neurological symptoms. The patient's strong conviction that symptoms represent MS limits insight into alternative explanations.

ground_truth.primary_diagnosis: Functional neurological disorder (functional weakness and sensory disturbance) superimposed on relapsing-remitting multiple sclerosis

differential.key_distinguishing: No new MRI lesions or enhancement on brain or spine imaging; bilateral symmetric pattern atypical for MS relapse (which is typically unilateral and follows anatomical pathways); positive functional signs (Hoover, give-way weakness, distractibility); fluctuating symptoms with emotional triggers.
SPMS progression is gradual over months to years, not a 2-week fluctuating course; MRI stable without progressive atrophy; patient's stable MRI over 3 years argues against active progression.
Spine MRI shows no new lesion, no cord compression, stable T6 lesion; bilateral symmetric pattern with positive functional signs is non-anatomical.
Give-way weakness and positive Hoover sign are specific for FND, not depression; dramatic fluctuation and normalization with distraction is not a feature of depressive psychomotor retardation.

optimal_actions.action: Obtain brain and spine MRI with gadolinium to rule out new MS inflammatory activity
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Stable MS lesions with no new or enhancing lesions, effectively excluding MS relapse

optimal_actions.action: Compare current MRI with prior studies to confirm stability
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Zero new lesions confirmed on formal comparison

optimal_actions.action: Perform detailed neurological examination with specific attention to functional signs, DISTINGUISHING new functional signs from old stable MS deficits
optimal_actions.expected_finding: New functional signs (Hoover sign, give-way weakness, tubular fields, midline splitting, gait inconsistency) on top of stable old MS findings (optic pallor, brisk reflexes, vibration reduction)

optimal_actions.action: Order labs including inflammatory markers to exclude active systemic process
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal labs, no evidence of active inflammation

optimal_actions.action: Consider SSEPs to assess whether sensory pathways have changed from baseline
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Cortical responses present (intact sensory pathway); old prolonged conduction stable

optimal_actions.action: Deliver the dual diagnosis transparently: 'Your MS is stable and this is not a relapse, but you have developed a different type of neurological problem called FND that can co-occur with MS'
Do NOT escalate MS immunotherapy — natalizumab should be continued as scheduled
Refer for psychological treatment addressing depression, health anxiety, and psychosocial stressors

critical_actions: Obtain brain and spine MRI to rule out new MS activity
Identify positive functional signs (Hoover sign, give-way weakness, distractibility, tubular fields) through careful examination
Clearly distinguish new functional symptoms from old stable MS deficits
Deliver the dual diagnosis transparently — FND can co-occur with MS
Do NOT escalate MS immunotherapy for functional symptoms
Treat underlying psychological factors (depression, health anxiety, psychosocial stressors)
contraindicated_actions: Treating with IV methylprednisolone for a 'pseudo-relapse' without MRI evidence of new inflammatory activity
Escalating MS therapy (switching from natalizumab to a more aggressive DMT) based on functional symptoms
Attributing all new symptoms to MS without examination for functional signs
Dismissing symptoms as 'just psychological' — FND is a legitimate neurological disorder that requires specific treatment
key_reasoning_points: FND co-occurs with MS at higher rates than the general population (up to 30% in some MS cohorts) — this is a well-recognized dual pathology
Positive functional signs (Hoover sign, give-way weakness, distractibility, tubular fields, midline splitting) are present ON TOP OF genuine stable MS findings
Stable MRI with no new or enhancing lesions excludes active MS relapse — this is the single most important investigation
The new symptoms are BILATERAL and symmetric — MS relapses are typically unilateral and follow anatomical pathways
Fluctuation with emotional stress and improvement with distraction are hallmarks of FND, not MS
Old stable MS findings (optic pallor, brisk reflexes, reduced vibration) should be carefully distinguished from new functional findings — these old signs do not indicate new disease activity
Psychosocial precipitants (fear of SPMS progression, marital stress, disability application) are risk factors for FND in MS patients
SSEPs showing intact cortical responses bilaterally argue against a new structural lesion causing the current sensory complaints

red_herrings.intended_effect: Every new neurological symptom in an MS patient is reflexively attributed to MS relapse or progression
red_herrings.correct_interpretation: MS patients can develop FND; positive functional signs on examination + stable MRI = FND, not relapse. The diagnosis of MS creates a strong cognitive anchor that must be overcome.

red_herrings.intended_effect: Suggests ongoing upper motor neuron disease activity that could explain the weakness
red_herrings.correct_interpretation: These are OLD findings from the prior thoracic myelitis 8 years ago, documented as stable on prior examinations. They are not new UMN signs and do not indicate current disease activity.

red_herrings.data_point: Fear of secondary progressive MS transition
red_herrings.intended_effect: Supports the assumption that MS is indeed progressing and these symptoms represent the transition to SPMS
red_herrings.correct_interpretation: This fear is a psychological precipitant for FND, not evidence of actual MS progression. The stable MRI and lack of gradual disability progression argue against SPMS transition.

metadata.diagnostic_challenge: FND superimposed on known MS — the ultimate dual pathology puzzle. Requires distinguishing new functional symptoms from stable old MS deficits, and overcoming the strong cognitive anchor that 'all new symptoms in MS = relapse.' Stable MRI is the key discriminating finding.
